items.title: Amebiasis: clinical keys and diagnosis
items.contentHtml:
Amebiasis due to Entamoeba histolytica

  • Entamoeba histolytica is the only pathogen of the genus; differentiate fromE. dispar(non-pathogenic) by antigen detection or PCR in feces.
  • Hematophagous trophozoite (with phagocytosed erythrocytes) is diagnostic of tissue invasion.
  • Clinical presentation: amoebic dysentery (stool with mucus and blood, tenesmus) vs non-dysenteric amoebic colitis; can produce amoebic liver abscess.
  • Diagnosis: direct examination of fresh stool (motile trophozoites), but requires serial sampling; positive serology (ELISA) in invasive disease.
  • First-line treatment: metronidazole 750 mg PO every 8 hours for 7-10 days for invasive colitis, followed by paromomycin 500 mg every 8 hours for 7 days to eliminate luminal cysts.

items.title: Giardiasis: diagnosis and management
items.contentHtml:
Giardiasis due to Giardia lamblia

  • Giardia lamblia (syn. G. intestinalis) causes watery diarrhea, steatorrhea, abdominal distension and malabsorption.
  • Pyriform trophozoite with two nuclei and suction disc; oval cyst with 4 nuclei, resistant to chlorine.
  • Diagnosis: serial stool examination (3 samples on alternate days) or ELISA for antigen in stool (sensitivity >90%).
  • First-line treatment: tinidazole 2 g single dose (preferred for better tolerance and efficacy); alternative metronidazole 250 mg PO every 8 hours for 5-7 days.
  • In case of resistance or therapeutic failure: nitazoxanide 500 mg every 12 hours for 3 days.

items.title: Cryptosporidium: cycle and transmission
items.contentHtml:
Life cycle of Cryptosporidium

  • Cryptosporidium parvum and C. hominisare the most common species; monoxene cycle (complete in a single host).
  • Infection due to ingestion of sporulated oocysts (chlorine-resistant). In the small intestine, they release sporozoites that invade epithelial cells.
  • Asexual multiplication (merogonia) produces merozoites; then sexual differentiation (gametogonia) forms oocysts that are eliminated in feces.
  • Oocysts with thick walls (autoinfection) and thin walls (environmental excretion). The cycle lasts 2-3 days.
  • Diagnosis: Modified Ziehl-Neelsen stain (acid-fast, red oocysts) or ELISA of stool antigen.

items.title: Dysentery: amoebic vs bacillary
items.contentHtml:
Differential diagnosis of amoebic dysentery

  • Amebic dysentery (Entamoeba histolytica): stool with mucus and blood, tenesmus, low or absent fever, subacute course. Hematophagous trophozoites in feces.
  • Bacillary dysentery (Shigella spp.): acute onset, high fever, colicky abdominal pain, stools with pus and blood, abundant fecal leukocytes. Positive stool culture.
  • Other causes: Campylobacter, Salmonella, Yersinia, E. coli enteroinvasive, pseudomembranous colitis due to C. difficile.
  • Key test: fresh stool examination (trophozoites with phagocytosed erythrocytes confirms invasive amebiasis).
  • Colonoscopy: “shirt button” ulcers in amebiasis; erythematous mucosa with exudate in shigellosis.

items.title: Metronidazole in amoebiasis: dose and schedule
items.contentHtml:
Treatment with metronidazole for amebiasis

  • Metronidazole is the drug of choice for invasive Entamoeba histolytica infection (colitis and liver abscess).
  • Dose in adults: 750 mg PO every 8 hours (or 500 mg intravenously if not tolerated orally) for 7-10 daysfor colitis; for liver abscess, 750 mg every 8 hours for 7-10 days.
  • Mechanism: reduction of the nitro group in anaerobes, forming toxic metabolites that damage parasite DNA.
  • Adverse effects: metallic taste, nausea, peripheral neuropathy (prolonged use); avoid alcohol (antabus effect).
  • After completing metronidazole, paromomycin (500 mg every 8 hours x 7 days) or iodoquinol should be administered to eliminate luminal cysts.

items.title: Cyst vs trophozoite: morphology and meaning
items.contentHtml:
Cyst vs trophozoite in feces

  • Trophozoite: active form, motile, with pseudopodia; It is observed in liquid or diarrheal stools. Indicates active and invasive infection (especially if it contains phagocytosed erythrocytes).
  • Cyst: resistance form, spherical, with thick wall; present in formed or semi-formed stools. Responsible for fecal-oral transmission.
  • In Entamoeba histolytica: trophozoite measures 10-60 µm, nucleus with central karyosome; Mature cyst has 4 nuclei and chromatoid bodies.
  • In Giardia lamblia: pyriform trophozoite with 2 nuclei; oval cyst with 4 nuclei.
  • Clinical significance: finding hematophagous trophozoites confirms invasive amebiasis; Cysts indicate carrier or luminal infection.

items.title: Cryptosporidiosis in human immunodeficiency virus and transplants
items.contentHtml:
Cryptosporidiosis in immunocompromised patients

  • In patients with human immunodeficiency virus (CD4 count <100 cells/µL) or immunosuppression by transplantation, Cryptosporidium causes chronic, profuse, watery diarrhea, with severe weight loss and malabsorption.
  • It can present as cholangitis. sclerosing or acalculous cholecystitis due to biliary involvement.
  • Diagnosis: modified Ziehl-Neelsen stain in stool; or antigen ELISA; in doubtful cases, duodenal biopsy with visualization of brush border parasites.
  • Treatment: nitazoxanide 500 mg every 12 hours for 3 days (limited efficacy in immunocompromised patients).
  • Management: optimize antiretroviral therapy in human immunodeficiency virus (CD4 recovery >100); in transplant, reduce immunosuppression if possible.

items.title: Cyclospora: cycle and diagnosis
items.contentHtml:
Life cycle of Cyclospora cayetanensis

  • Cyclospora cayetanensis is a coccidia that causes prolonged watery diarrhea (weeks) with fatigue and anorexia.
  • Monoxene cycle: ingestion of sporulated oocysts (in contaminated food or water). In the small intestine, they release sporozoites that invade enterocytes.
  • Asexual and sexual multiplication within the epithelium; Non-sporulated oocysts are shed in feces and require sporulation in the environment (days to weeks) to be infective.
  • Diagnosis: modified Ziehl-Neelsen stain (acid-fast oocysts, 8-10 µm, with blue autofluorescence under UV light).
  • Treatment: trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) 160/800 mg every 12 hours for 7-10 days; alternative for allergy sufferers: nitazoxanide.

items.title: Balantidiasis: zoonoses and management
items.contentHtml:
Balantidiasis due to Balantidium coli

  • Balantidium coli is the largest ciliated protozoan that infects humans; main reservoir: pigs.
  • Fecal-oral transmission due to ingestion of cysts; In the colon, trophozoites invade the mucosa, causing ulcers similar to amoebiasis.
  • Clinical symptoms: mucous-bloody diarrhea, tenesmus, abdominal pain; It can be asymptomatic or severe (fulminant dysentery).
  • Diagnosis: fresh stool examination (large trophozoites, 50-100 µm, ciliated, with kidney-shaped macronucleus).
  • First-line treatment: tetracycline 500 mg PO every 6 hours for 10 days; alternative: metronidazole 750 mg every 8 hours for 5-7 days.

items.title: ELISA in giardiasis: usefulness and precision
items.contentHtml:
Diagnosis of giardiasis with ELISA

  • ELISA for detection of Giardia lamblia antigen in feces has sensitivity >90% and specificity >95%, superior to serial microscopic examination.
  • Detects antigens of trophozoites and cysts; useful in single samples, especially in patients with chronic diarrhea or malabsorption.
  • Does not require serial samples; can be performed on formed or liquid stools.
  • Limitations: false positives in recently treated infections (residual antigen); does not differentiate between Giardia and other parasites.
  • Test of choice in outbreaks or epidemiological screening; confirm with microscopy if symptoms are very suggestive and ELISA negative.

items.title: Tinidazole in giardiasis: single dose
items.contentHtml:
Treatment of giardiasis with tinidazole

  • Tinidazole is a second-generation nitroimidazole, with a long half-life (12-14 h), allowing a single dose.
  • Dose in adults: 2 g PO single dose (in children: 50 mg/kg, maximum 2 g).
  • Efficacy comparable or superior to metronidazole (cure >90%), with fewer adverse effects (less nausea and metallic taste).
  • Mechanism: similar to metronidazole, but with greater tissue penetration and lower resistance.
  • Contraindications: pregnancy (first trimester) and lactation (excretion in milk); avoid alcohol for 72 hours postdose.

items.title: Prevention: water, food and habits
items.contentHtml:
Prevention of protozoan infections

  • Key measures: washing hands with soap and water after going to the bathroom and before eating; avoid consumption of untreated water (especially in endemic areas).
  • For Cryptosporidium and Giardia: boil water (>1 minute) or use <1 µm pore filters; Chlorine does not inactivate Cryptosporidium cysts.
  • Wash and peel fruits/vegetables; Avoid raw or poorly cooked foods in risk areas.
  • In travelers: do not consume ice, drinks from unsafe sources, or street food.
  • In immunocompromised people: in addition to the previous measures, avoid contact with animal feces (especially pigs for Balantidium).
